entry.title: How SAP Business AI Drives Sustainability in Automotive Manufacturing
entry.content:
Sustainability is no longer optional—it’s a business necessity. SAP Business AI helps companies integrate eco-friendly practices by analyzing environmental impact and suggesting sustainable alternatives.

Take Martur Fompak International (MFI), a leader in automotive seat production. MFI’s innovative solution uses SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to suggest sustainable fabric alternatives for seat covers. By analyzing their catalog with AI, MFI helps customers and internal teams compare materials based on carbon impact, enabling data-driven sustainability choices. Read the full story here.

Key benefits of SAP Business AI for sustainability include:

  • AI-driven recommendations for sustainable materials.
  • Carbon impact tracking to compare environmental footprints.
  • Seamless integration into business operations for long-term sustainability.

Summary 

  • SAP Business AI helps businesses analyze environmental impact and identify sustainable alternatives.
  • MFI uses AI to suggest eco-friendly fabrics and measure carbon footprints in automotive seat production.
  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management provides data-driven insights for informed decision-making.
  • AI integration embeds sustainability into core business operations for long-term impact.

entry.title: Prefilling a Value Help Filter from the Parent Entity in RAP
entry.content:
In a RAP application, I recently encountered a common requirement: pre‑filter a Value Help on a child entity field using a value coming from the parent entity (in my case, the CompanyCode from the header).

The scenario looks simple, but RAP currently cannot evaluate an association inside an additionalBinding. I tried the expected annotation:

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [
  {
    entity: { name : 'I_PurchaseOrderAPI01', element : 'PurchaseOrder' },
    additionalBinding: [
      { element: 'CompanyCode', localElement: '_Header.CompanyCode', usage: #FILTER }
    ]
  }
]
PurchaseOrder;
Unfortunately, the framework does not resolve the _Header association, and the filter is not applied.
There is an even more fundamental issue: When creating a child record, the child entity simply does not exist yet.  So you cannot push CompanyCode into the child entity and bind from there—there’s no child instance at that moment.
Because of this, annotations alone cannot solve the problem.
 

Workaround: Inject the Filter from a Fiori Elements Controller Extension

 The most pragmatic solution is to extend the Object Page controller and add the filter programmatically when the Value Help opens. This avoids backend limitations and works well during create flows.

Why my case is simpler

In my application, the PurchaseOrder field is a multi‑input on the header, not inside a table. That means:

  • There is one single generated field,
  • Easy to identify,
  • Easy to attach the opened event to its Value Help.

For tables, the logic is still possible but more dynamic (details below).

Implementation – Header Multi‑Input Scenario

Step 1 — Add a controller extension

In the Object Page, add a controller extension (via the page map in Fiori Tools).

 

The generated extension usually provides an onInit method override. Insert a call to your custom method:
onInit: function () {
  this._extendPurchaseOrderValueHelp();
}

Step 2 — Locate the generated field + attach a handler

Use Chrome DevTools to retrieve the generated ID for the multi‑input field. Then:

_extendPurchaseOrderVH: function () {

            const oView = this.base.getView();

            // 1) Get generated MDC field for PurchaseOrder
            const sPurchaseOrderId = "my.app::DocumentObjectPage--fe::FormContainer::FileTemplate::FormElement::DataField::_TemplatePurchaseOrder::PurchaseOrder::MultiValueField"; 
            let oField = oView.byId(sPurchaseOrderId);
            if (!oField) {
                return; // not rendered yet
            }

            // 2) Get MDC value help attach method to opened event
            const sPurchaseOrderValueHelpId = oField.getValueHelp && oField.getValueHelp(); // can return an id
            const oPurchaseOrderValueHelp = sPurchaseOrderValueHelpId ? oView.byId(sPurchaseOrderValueHelpId) : null;
            if (!oPurchaseOrderValueHelp) {
                return;
            }
            oPurchaseOrderValueHelp.attachOpened(this._onOpenPurchaseOrderValueHelp);

        }

This function:

  1. Retrieves the generated MDC field,
  2. Retrieves its Value Help,
  3. Hooks into the opened event.

Step 3 — Inject the parent’s CompanyCode into the Value Help

        _onOpenPurchaseOrderValueHelp: function (oEvent) {

            const oDocument = oEvent.getSource().getBindingContext().getObject();
            const oContainer = oEvent.getParameter("container");

            const aContents = oContainer.getContent ? oContainer.getContent() : [];
            aContents.forEach((oContent) => {
                if (oContent.getFilterBar) {
                    const oFilterBar = oContent.getFilterBar();
                    if (oFilterBar?.setFilterConditions) {
                        const mCond = oFilterBar.getFilterConditions ? oFilterBar.getFilterConditions() : {};
                        mCond.CompanyCode = [{
                            operator: "EQ",
                            values: [oDocument.CompanyCode]
                        }];
                        oFilterBar.setFilterConditions(mCond);
                    }
                }
            });

        }

A note about the table (item) scenario

In many applications, the Value Help is not in the header but inside a table row. This is actually the more common case.

However, the logic becomes a bit different because:

  • Each row generates its own instance of the field,
  • These controls appear dynamically as the table renders,
  • IDs are not static like a header field,
  • You must find each row’s field and attach the event individually,
  • You cannot rely on a fixed ID.

This makes the implementation slightly more complex, but still entirely doable.

entry.title: Re: Creating Simulation Template with start and end pages
entry.content:
hi 

✅ 1. Ensure the Start and End Pages themselves are published

SAP clarified that Start and End pages are separate content objects and must be explicitly published.
If they are not published individually, they will show up blank in Companion or other outputs.

“The Start and End Pages are separate content objects so must be published along with the Simulation.”
[community.sap.com]

✔ What to do:

  1. Go to the Book Page objects linked to the simulation.
  2. Right–click each Start Page and End Page.
  3. Select Publish.
  4. Re-publish the Simulation afterward.

✅ 2. Check whether the Template itself became corrupted

SAP notes that if the template used to generate Start/End pages is published, but pages appear blank, this could indicate a deeper issue.

“If the templates used to create the Start and End pages are published, please raise a support ticket… I cannot think of any other reason the page appears without content.”
[community.sap.com]

This aligns with your statement:

“It worked for a while… now all start and end pages appear blank.”

Templates can become corrupted especially if:

  • Styles or inherited objects were edited
  • The base template was renamed, moved, or unpublished
  • The linked pages were inadvertently modified

✅ 3. Test by creating a new simulation using the same template

This will reveal whether the template is the cause, not the individual simulations.

✔ Steps:

  1. Create a new blank simulation.
  2. Apply the same Simulation Template you previously used.
  3. Preview it in Producer and publish it.

🧪 Interpretation:

  • If Start/End pages are also blank → the template is broken.
  • If they appear normally → the issue is with your existing project objects.

✅ 4. If your template is corrupted, recreate it

You asked:

“If I have to create a new simulation template, how can I link it to existing simulations?”

Unfortunately, existing simulations can’t automatically swap to a new template.
You will need to manually insert the new Start/End pages into each affected simulation.

✔ To insert new pages:

  1. Open your simulation in Producer.
  2. Insert → Template Slide (the new Start or End Page).
    (SAP documentation confirms you can add template slides into already-created simulations.)
    [linkedin.com]
  3. Delete the blank/corrupted ones.
  4. Publish all objects again.

✅ 5. When all else fails: raise an SAP Support Ticket

If nothing fixes it:

"Please raise an SAP support ticket… use Component ID KM‑SEN‑MGR."
[community.sap.com]

 

Regards

Surjith

entry.title: Re: Joule - Performance and Goals Agent
entry.content:
hi

Recommended Steps to Try Now

✔ 1. Confirm activation prerequisites

Ensure PM, GM, and/or CPM modules are actually active and contain data—not just “enabled.”

✔ 2. Re-check permissions

The manager must have the explicit permission:
AI Access → Performance & Goals Agent.

✔ 3. Manually trigger sync jobs

Run:

  • SF – Refresh Synthetic Group Data
  • IPS – User/Group Sync

This forces data to propagate to Joule.

✔ 4. Re-run CDM refresh

From SAP BTP Work Zone content manager.

✔ 5. Validate trusted domain setup

Misconfiguration can cause Joule to “see” no contextual data.

✔ 6. Test with multiple manager profiles

Sometimes individual users don’t have sufficient role exposure for Joule to understand their team hierarchy.

Regards

Surjith

entry.title: Re: No access to EarlyWatch alerts for end customers
entry.content:
In order for partner S-users to view end customers in the the Service Messages or SAP EarlyWatch Alert Workspace applications, their S-user ID needs to be given Service Reports (Partner) authorization. If a user is missing this authorization, use the self-service authorization request according to KBA 2891528 or contact an administrator listed on the My Important Contacts.
For further guidance, refer to the KBAs below: 

2929232 - SAP Partner cannot access end customer numbers in SAP EarlyWatch / Service Messages - SAP for Me
2627899 - How do VAR-delivered support partners check the EarlyWatch Alert (EWA) report for their end customers - SAP for Me

entry.title: Re: How to add a Super Administrator
entry.content:
As outlined in KBA 2596214 - How to maintain Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs listed in My Important Contacts - SAP for Me
Customers are responsible for maintaining their own S-users and their own Administrators listed in the Important Contacts page as explained in KBA 1271517. For security reasons, SAP can only assist with adding an administrator when no administrators are currently available.
To add a Super Administrator, an existing Super Administrator must grant all necessary authorizations to another S-user ID. You can refer to KBA 1614304 for detailed instructions on how to copy or clone authorizations. I recommend contacting one of the existing Super Administrators listed in the https://me.sap.com/userscontacts/impcont to request the required authorization for your S-user ID

entry.title: Introducing Support for Different Time Granularities in Remote Models with Advanced Formulas
entry.content:
Introduction

As planning scenarios become increasingly complex, the need for flexible, high‑performance calculation logic has never been greater. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) continues to evolve to meet this demand, and the latest enhancements to Advanced Formulas introduce new levels of power and simplicity to model‑driven planning.

In this release, we are excited to introduce a key enhancement that streamlines common calculations, reduces script complexity, and expands what planners can achieve within the Data Action Script step. This blog highlights what's new, how it works, and how you can begin leveraging it in your planning models.

 

Support for Different Time-Granularity for Remote Models

One of the most impactful enhancements in this release is the new Support for Different Time‑Granularity for Remote Models.

Previously, remote models were constrained by the time granularity defined in the default model. This meant that the time granularity used in Advanced Formulas had to align precisely with both the source and the default model structure, often limiting planners when business requirements demanded more flexible or diverse time‑based calculations.

With this enhancement, Advanced Formulas now supports working seamlessly across multiple time granularities, even when retrieving data directly from remote models. This enables:

  • More flexible planning scenarios
  • Richer time‑series calculations
  • Better alignment between operational and financial planning processes
 

Key Benefits

More Natural Alignment with Business Cycles

Different planning functions operate at different rhythms. Financial planning typically uses monthly or quarterly cycles, while operational areas, such as Sales or Workforce, may rely heavily on weekly or daily views. Support for different time granularities allows remote models to reflect these variations naturally.

Enhanced Advanced Formula Logic

Advanced Formulas can now reference, calculate, and iterate across different time granularities without additional modeling workarounds. This significantly simplifies complex scripting and reduces the need for auxiliary aggregation models.

 

How It Works

This enhancement introduces the following capabilities:

  • Linked models can expose multiple levels of time granularity.
  • Advanced Formulas can reference time periods with higher or lower granularity than the default model.
  • The LINK() function now works reliably even when time granularities between models differ.

This ensures that time‑based calculations behave consistently across remote and planning models.

 

Practical Use Cases: Weekly Sales Data to Monthly Revenue Calculation

 

One powerful example of using this capability is referencing Weekly Sales data within a Monthly Financial Planning model. This ensures that planners maintain the precision of granular sales data while performing aggregated monthly revenue calculations.

 

Business Scenario Overview

Weekly Sales Model

  • Sales Quantity
  • Unit Price
  • Weekly date granularity

Monthly Financial Planning Model

  • Requires monthly Revenue values for forecasting and profitability analysis

 

Financial Model(Month)

 

Sales Model(Week)

 

Date Dimension(Week)

 

Source Data(Sales Model)

  • Weekly Total Revenue = Σ(Quantity × Price) = 1,000.00

 

Advanced Formulas Script

MEMBERSET [d/Measures] = "Amount"
MEMBERSET [d/Date] = "202601" TO "202612"
MEMBERSET [d/Entity_D] = "US"
MEMBERSET [d/BusinessUnit] = "BU_SKU"
MEMBERSET [d/ProductGroup_D] = "SKU"

MODEL [Sales Planning]
	// Product_Item and Sales_Channel only exist in the linked source model Sales but not in default model Financial. They are used to define the source model scope.
	MEMBERSET [d/Product_Item] = ("SKU_001", "SKU_002", "SKU_003", "SKU_004", "SKU_005")
	MEMBERSET [d/Channel_Sales] = ("Direc", "Online", "Retail")
	//Since the Date dimension is not a shared dimension, you need to define the Date scope of the source model.
	MEMBERSET [d/Date] = "202601" TO "202652"
ENDMODEL

// 33001000 stands for Account: Revenue Domestic-Product. 
// The granularities of the Date dimensions are different, adding syntax " [d/Date] = PERIOD([Sales Planning].[d/Date] " to accommodate for the difference.
DATA([d/Account_Fin] = "33001000", [d/DataSrc] = "DataSrc_Sales", [d/Date] = PERIOD([Sales Planning].[d/Date]))
= LINK([Sales Planning], [d/Measures] = "S90007000", [d/Version] = "public.Plan01")
* LINK([Sales Planning], [d/Measures] = "S90008000", [d/Version] = "public.Plan01")
  • When referencing data between models that use different time granularities (for example, weekly source → monthly target), you must explicitly align the time dimension in the DATA() statement. Use the PERIOD() function to map the source model’s time member to the target model’s time context.
     
    DATA(.., [d/Date] = PERIOD([Sales Planning].[d/Date]), ...) = LINK(...)

 

Result: Calculated Monthly Revenue

  • January (4 weeks): 4,000.00
  • February (4 weeks): 4,000.00
  • March (5 weeks): 5,000.00
     
 
 

Conclusion

This enhancement to time‑granularity handling significantly improves the flexibility and maintainability of planning scenarios in SAP Analytics Cloud. By enabling Advanced Formulas to reference weekly (or daily) data within a monthly model, organizations can plan more accurately and make better‑informed decisions.

We hope this new capability helps streamline your planning processes.
If you have feedback or want to share your own use cases, we invite you to join the conversation in the SAP Community.

 
Moonjun

entry.title: Re: SAP Project Systems -BAPIs (A quick overview)
entry.content:
@Abhishek_Band  - There are no dedicated standard BAPIs specifically designed for reading overall, annual current budget, budget released, and budget assigned values in SAP Project Systems. However, there are alternative approaches available.

Alternative Approaches

Function Modules

While specific BAPIs are not available, you can explore these alternatives:

  • Function Module Approach: Search for function modules in SE80 or SE37 using patterns like: BAPI_PROJECT_* , *BUDGET* ,*PS_*

  • Custom Development Approach

    Since standard BAPIs are not available, consider creating a custom function module with a developer

entry.title: Standard Workflows in SAP PPM
entry.content:
Core Workflow Categories

Project Management Workflows
  • Project status change workflows for tasks, checklist items, phases, and projects.
  • Project element approval workflows triggered when project leaders release elements ,
  • Change management workflows for network modifications and configuration changes ,
Portfolio Management Workflows
  • Item deletion workflow (WS00800003)
  • Item attribute change workflow (WS00800004)
  • Item status change workflow (WS00800008)
  • Item decision point workflows for status and attribute changes
Resource Management Workflows
  • Purchase order change workflow (TS20000653) for material components and external processing 
  • Confirmation workflows for entering actual data (TS00007944) 
  • Deviation workflows triggered when duration or work exceeds predefined values 
Financial Management Workflows
  • Initiative deletion workflow (WS00700032)
  • Initiative attribute change workflow (WS00700022)
  • Initiative status change workflow (WS00700021)
  • Initiative decision point workflows for approvals and changes

Workflow Details

Item Status Change Workflow (WS00800008)
  • Purpose: Automates notifications when portfolio item status changes occur.
  • Trigger: Status modification of portfolio items
  • Steps: Status validation, recipient determination, notification generation
  • Participants: Item owners, approvers, and stakeholders with relevant authorizations
  • Outcome: Automated email notifications with links to affected portfolio items
Configuration Change Management Workflow (WS20000265)
  • Purpose: Manages configuration changes in sales orders with network conflicts 
  • Trigger: Configuration change creating manual adjustment requirements 
  • Steps: Display change management, create text, display text, make change 
  • Participants: Dispatcher, project planning employees, MRP controllers
  • Outcome: Systematic resolution of configuration conflicts ,
Purchase Order Change Workflow (TS20000653)
  • Purpose: Handles changes to purchase orders for material components 
  • Trigger: Modifications to dates, quantities, customers, vendors, or addresses in networks 
  • Steps: Change detection, notification to purchasing, direct purchase order modification 
  • Participants: Purchasing department personnel 
  • Outcome: Updated purchase orders reflecting project changes 

Configuration Considerations

  • Workflow activation through PPM Customizing under Portfolio Management → Global Customizing → Global Settings → Workflow Settings
  • BAdI implementation activation for attribute change workflows (RPM_PROJ_CUST_WF for items, /RPM/DES_POINT_WF for decision points)
  • Web server alias configuration for portal links in notifications
  • Standard task assignment to authorized agents and event link activation 

Best Practices

  • Configure workflow recipients based on authorization activities (admin, read, write) to ensure proper notification distribution 
  • Ensure user email addresses are properly maintained for successful notification delivery
  • Use standard workflow IDs provided by SAP before creating custom workflows 
  • Monitor workflow performance using transaction SBWP to track started workflows 
  • Leverage function group /RPM/WORKFLOW for debugging and troubleshooting workflow issues

entry.title: Re: Contingent Worker/External to Employee Conversions th...
entry.content:
@tracymca 

One approach that can be followed is: 

  1. As an active or terminated Contingent Worker, the applicant can be added to Onboarding either through manual Onboarding, Recruiting, or external ATS systems and follows the standard Onboarding process.
  2. User receives email with login credentials for the future employment and performs Personal Data Collection.
  3. After Personal Data Collection, the 2nd Rehire check will initiate. This action will trigger because enough rehire match data has been identified.
  4. Within the rehire verification match, all inactive, active employees and active employees will future dated terminations who match the rehire criteria will appear in the rehire verification step.
  5. After the user has been identified as a Rehire on New Employment, the user can login via existing login credentials from the active Contingent Employment, or new login credentials for the future employment.
  6. The user can switch between the current (Active) and future (Inactive) profiles through the Context Switcher to complete necessary tasks.
  7. The user must be on the Inactive profile in order to complete the remaining Onboarding tasks (Rehire Data Review, Compliance forms, etc.)

Additional Information:

  • This includes support of a time gap between the termination date and start date (ie: Contract ends on Friday and full time position starts Monday).
  • If the contingent worker’s contract end date has not been entered, this will need to be completed in Employee Central, before the rehire verification can be completed.
    • To enter the contract end date, navigate to Employee Central and update the date, once the date has been entered, the rehire verification step will reflect the update and allow for action to be taken.
  • Rehire with New Employment button will be available for selection allowing the contingent worker to be converted to a regular employee and the rest of the onboarding process flow will continue.

KBA reference - 3125911 - How to Hire a Contingent Worker - Onboarding - SAP for Me

I hope this helps!

 

Regards,

Tabassum

entry.title: Re: SAP Certification update: Practical exams roll out in 2026
entry.content:
Hello @DianaRoesner, I’ve since retaken the End-to-End Business Processes practical exam and also attempted the S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Financial Accounting practical exam.

Unfortunately, in both cases I encountered system issues that prevented progression beyond the second task. Support tickets were logged during the exam window, but the issues could not be resolved before time elapsed.

As a result, both exams are now recorded as failed and two exam attempt credits have been consumed, despite the failures being caused by unresolved system defects rather than candidate performance.

I’m raising this to seek guidance on the appropriate remediation path (recredit and invalidation of attempts) and to understand how candidates should proceed when system blockers occur during active practical exams.

entry.title: Re: Triggering Australian Compliance Forms for New Hires...
entry.content:
Hi @karunakarsadu ,

You can use FieldMetaOverrideConfiguration to override configuration in global filter used for assignment of compliance forms to the user.

You can create a custom field to store the Home country of the candidate and set the entity path accordingly. 

Please ensure you are selecting the appropriate meta override type: 

Global Filter Metadata Override - This meta override type is used to assign compliance forms to an address type other than the default address type.

Form Metadata Override - This meta override type is used to override the new hire personal data reporting in form. Enter EC_META_CONFIG as the ID for the configuration to be global and applicable to all forms. When using this ID, you can leave the supportedTypes field blank.

Guide Link - Setting Up Compliance Form Assignment Using Override Configuration | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: Business data validation using DTV tool

entry.title: Roadmap Inquiry: UI5 Table Grouping Expand/Collapse for OData V4
entry.content:
Hi,
Our SBN team has encountered an issue during the development of the table group function for the catalog. The data volume of our catalog table will be extremely large, and we need to group the data by customer. However, there is currently no available control to support this functionality.
 
We would like to inquire: When does our UI5 control library plan to support the expand/collapse functionality for grouping?
 
The Analytical Table can fulfill the requirement, but it does not support OData V4. We will not revert to the older OData V2 protocol just to compensate for this missing frontend feature.
Meanwhile, the Tree Table is not suitable for our use case.
As a result, we currently do not have an appropriate control to support expand and collapse for group functionality.
 
The Fiori Elements Framework ALV supports the latest OData V4, but migrating to this solution involves cost.
Therefore, we would like to know if there is an upgrade roadmap for the UI5 Table group expand/collapse function.
 
Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Feature Activation Hub in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
Introduction

With Service/Sales Cloud V2, SAP now offers a capability called Feature Activation Hub, which allows you as an administrator to view and activate new features, before they are made generally available.

We will explore this new capability in this blog.

Access Feature activation Hub

     To access the Feature Activation Hub, you need to add the required Service & App to your business role as shown below:

  1. Go to System Settings  -> Users and Control  - > Roles
     

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

        2.  Add the business service CustomerFeatureToggleService to your administrator business role.

     3. Enable  Customer Features  app.

Activate New Features

   You can now  view and activate new features before the features are activated by default.

  1.   Go to System Settings  -> System Preferences  - > Feature Activation Hub

    2. You can view the details of the new features which are available for activation before they are activated by default in all systems.

Let's look at the different options on activation page:

       A.  Quick filter by Available Date & Service.

       B.  Explore all new features in Help Portal.

      C. Sort by Available Date, Activation date & Service

      D.  Get quick information about the specific feature, you can click on See More to get more details in What's New viewer on the SAP Help Portal.

    

E.  Once you have enough information and you think the new feature will help your organization boost productivity, you can activate the new feature by selecting the Activate checkbox.

Important information

  • Once you activate a feature, you can't deactivate it. It is  recommended that you activate new features in your test or development tenants before activating them in your production tenant.
  • New features listed in Feature Activation Hub are activated by default after the stated period of time. You can only opt-in early if you want.

entry.title: Re: Remove old PID / unlink old email from SAP Account Ma...
entry.content:
Hello customer,

Yes now customer is able to unlink user(s) from the UID on customer side via self-service.

Kindly refer to the detailed steps documented in KBA 3619621 - How to unlink a user from an SAP Universal ID in SAP Account Manager?

But kindly know users cannot delete an SAP Universal ID directly through Self-Service. Please submit a case under component XX-SER-UID to request a UID completely deleted on SAP side. Refer to KBA 3539768.

In case you encounter any issue while unlinking user from UID, also feel free to submit a case under XX-SER-UID with the issue details.

Hope this helps.

Thanks and regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Adding filter to a measure variable
entry.content: Filtering isn’t working because measures return calculated values, not raw fields. If you want to filter by remarks like keep listed, delist, or review product, move that logic into a calculated column/dimension instead of a measure. Columns can be filtered directly, while measures are better for aggregations. Also check that the values are consistent (no blanks or mismatched text). This way your filter will recognize and apply correctly.

entry.title: Re: G/L account for Vietnam rollout implementation
entry.content:
Hi koki7,

Please see the recommendation below, 

If you find my response helpful, kindly accept the answer and award a Kudo. This motivates me to serve you better.

Current Procedure Review

Step 1: Vietnamese Accounting Standards Compliance

Your approach of obtaining G/L accounts according to Vietnamese Accounting Standards is correct and aligns with regulatory requirements [1],[2],[3]. Vietnam follows Circular 200/2014/TT-BTC which defines the standard chart of accounts structure with specific account codes for different asset, liability, equity, revenue, and expense categories [1],[2].

Step 2: YAVN Chart of Accounts Setup

Your manual addition of Vietnamese accounts to YAVN is a valid approach. The fact that default YAVN codes don't match Vietnamese standards is expected, as YCOA is designed as a global chart of accounts for all countries and is not country-specific [4]. You can create new G/L accounts by copying existing ones and modifying them to meet Vietnamese requirements [5],[6].

Step 3: YAVN to YCOA Mapping Strategy

Mapping YAVN codes to YCOA through the alternative G/L account section is the correct configuration approach [7]. This allows you to maintain Vietnamese account codes while leveraging SAP's standard account determination and reporting structures.

Gap Analysis: Unmappable YAVN Accounts

Identification Process

When no suitable YCOA account exists for mapping a YAVN account, you should:

  • Analyze the business purpose and account type of the unmappable YAVN account
  • Review Vietnamese regulatory requirements for that specific account
  • Determine if the account represents a unique Vietnamese business requirement
Resolution Options

  1. Create New YCOA Accounts: Copy an existing similar YCOA account and modify it to match the Vietnamese requirement. Ensure you follow SAP's numbering conventions to avoid conflicts with future SAP updates [8].

  2. Extend Existing YCOA Structure: Add sub-accounts within existing YCOA ranges. For example, if you need multiple revenue accounts, create them within the 40000000-49999999 range [4].

  3. Use Account Determination Groups: Configure account determination to automatically post to appropriate YCOA accounts based on business transactions, even if direct mapping isn't possible.

  4. Reclassify YAVN Accounts: Consider whether some Vietnamese accounts can be consolidated or reclassified to fit existing YCOA structures without losing regulatory compliance.

Recommended Approach

For unmappable YAVN accounts, create new YCOA accounts by copying similar existing accounts [5],[6]. When creating new accounts:

  • Use available number ranges within YCOA structure [4]
  • Maintain all relevant configurations including account determination
  • Ensure proper account type assignment [9]
  • Document changes in your configuration for future reference [8]

Configuration Validation Checklist

  • YAVN Account Completeness: Verify all Vietnamese Accounting Standards accounts are included [1],[2]
  • Mapping Accuracy: Ensure each YAVN account has appropriate YCOA mapping or justification for unmapped accounts
  • Account Determination: Validate that automatic account determination works correctly with your new accounts [5]
  • Financial Statement Integration: Confirm accounts appear correctly in financial reporting structures [10]
  • Translation Maintenance: Set up proper account descriptions in Vietnamese language [11]

Potential Issues & Mitigation

Common Configuration Risks
  • Risk 1: Incomplete Mapping: Some Vietnamese-specific accounts may not have direct YCOA equivalents
  • Risk 2: Regulatory Non-compliance: Incorrect mapping could affect Vietnamese tax and statutory reporting
  • Risk 3: Future SAP Updates: New SAP releases might introduce conflicts with custom account numbers [8]
Preventive Measures
  • Document all custom accounts and mappings thoroughly
  • Test all account determination scenarios before go-live
  • Validate Vietnamese statutory reports with your local accounting team
  • Reserve number ranges for future Vietnamese-specific accounts

Next Steps

  • Complete the mapping exercise for all YAVN accounts
  • Create necessary new YCOA accounts for unmappable items using the copy function [6]
  • Test account determination and posting scenarios
  • Validate financial statement presentation meets Vietnamese requirements
  • Prepare user training materials showing both YAVN and YCOA account references

entry.title: Re: Limitations with RFQ and Supplier Quotations
entry.content:
Good day @Astrid_Borhaug 

Thank you for your question.

What you described is one of the limitations and known behaviour in public cloud. When managing RFQs, partial quotation with fewer line items is not supported and it does not allow suppliers to delete RFQ items from the quotation. This is also defined  in the attached feature scope https://help.sap.com/doc/7c9e0bbbd1664c2581b2038a1c7ae4b3/2602.500/en-US/FSD_CE2602.pdf

I recommend you design RFQs in smaller logical groups, make sure you include possible substitute items as separate lines before you publish it and reissue forces RFQs for items that were not covered after your supplier responses.

You may also consider influencing these functions for the future, you can raise an SAP influence request as this is a known product limitation.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Actual Overhead Calculation for Projects (BNA) - No E...
entry.content:
Hi byd,

Please check the following possible root cause analysis, If you find my response helpful, kindly accept the answer and award a Kudo. This motivates me to serve you better.


Prerequisites Validation

  1. Project Master Data

    • Verify project is active and properly structured with WBS elements [1]
    • Confirm project allows cost postings and overhead calculations
    • Check if settlement profile is assigned with overhead calculation settings

  2. Costing Sheet Configuration (SSCUI 102922)

    • Validate overhead rate base "Material" matches actual cost elements posted [2]
    • Ensure calculation sequence and dependencies are correct
    • Verify credit cost element assignment for overhead allocation
    • Check if costing sheet is activated for the correct controlling area

  3. Cost Element Group Setup

    • Confirm cost element group 1200_CE contains the actual cost elements posted to project
    • Validate that posted primary costs belong to cost elements within group 1200_CE
    • Ensure cost element categories allow overhead calculation

  4. Project Assignment Configuration

    • Critical Gap: Projects in SAP S/4HANA Cloud may not have direct costing sheet assignment like cost centers [3]
    • Settlement profile must contain overhead calculation parameters
    • Verify if project type supports overhead calculation functionality

Diagnostic Steps

Step 1: Costing Sheet Validation
  1. Navigate to SSCUI 102922 "Define Costing Sheets for Overhead Calculation" [2]
  2. Open costing sheet Y001 and verify:
    • Overhead rate percentage is correctly defined
    • Base selection criteria matches "Material" cost elements
    • Credit cost element is specified for allocation posting
    • Calculation dependencies and sequence are logical
Step 2: Project Configuration Check
  1. Access project master data through relevant Fiori app
  2. Verify settlement profile assignment includes overhead settings
  3. Check if project structure allows overhead allocation
  4. Validate project status permits cost postings and calculations
Step 3: Cost Element Analysis
  1. Review actual cost postings on the project
  2. Verify posted cost elements are included in cost element group 1200_CE
  3. Check if posted amounts meet minimum thresholds for overhead calculation
  4. Ensure posting period aligns with overhead calculation period
Step 4: Job Parameter Verification
  1. Confirm job selection criteria includes your specific project
  2. Verify fiscal period matches the period of primary cost postings
  3. Check company code 1510 scope in job parameters
  4. Validate ledger 0L assignment is correct

Common Configuration Gaps

Missing Assignments
  • Key Issue: Unlike cost centers, projects may not have direct costing sheet assignment capability [3]
  • Settlement profile missing overhead calculation activation
  • Cost element group 1200_CE may not include all relevant cost elements
  • Project structure may not support overhead allocation
Configuration Errors
  • Overhead rate base "Material" may not match actual posted cost element types
  • Missing or incorrect credit cost element in costing sheet Y001
  • Calculation sequence issues preventing overhead computation
  • Period/date misalignment between postings and calculation run

Resolution Steps

Immediate Actions
  1. Verify Project Type Compatibility: Confirm your project type supports overhead calculation - this functionality may be limited to specific project types in S/4HANA Cloud [3]
  2. Check Settlement Profile: Ensure settlement profile assigned to project has overhead calculation activated
  3. Validate Cost Element Mapping: Verify that cost elements in posted transactions are included in cost element group 1200_CE
  4. Review Costing Sheet Assignment: Determine how costing sheet Y001 is linked to your project (may be through settlement profile or project type configuration)
Configuration Corrections
  1. Update Settlement Profile: Add overhead calculation parameters to project's settlement profile
  2. Modify Cost Element Group: Ensure group 1200_CE includes all relevant cost elements from your project postings
  3. Correct Costing Sheet: Verify Y001 has proper credit cost element and calculation logic
  4. Adjust Project Configuration: Enable overhead calculation flags in project master data if available
Testing Validation
  1. Execute overhead calculation job with test run first to validate configuration
  2. Check for any warning messages or calculation results in test mode
  3. If test run shows expected results, execute in live mode
  4. Verify allocation postings appear in project cost reports

Verification Checklist

Pre-Execution Validation
  •  Costing sheet Y001 properly configured with correct base and rates [2]
  •  Cost element group 1200_CE contains cost elements from actual project postings
  •  Project has settlement profile with overhead calculation enabled
  •  Primary costs posted to project in correct period and company code
Post-Execution Verification
  •  Overhead allocations posted to project (check via project cost reports)
  •  Cost reports show allocated overhead amounts
  •  BNA test script section 4.1 requirements met [1]
  •  No error messages in job log and allocation documents created

Next Steps

  1. Investigate Project Type Limitations: Verify if BNA scope item supports overhead calculation for your specific project type [3]
  2. Review Settlement Profile Configuration: Ensure proper overhead settings in project settlement profile
  3. Test with Alternative Configuration: Consider using different costing sheet assignment method if direct assignment not available
  4. Consult SAP Documentation: Review specific S/4HANA Cloud limitations for project overhead calculation in BNA scope

Important Note: Based on SAP community feedback, overhead calculation functionality may have specific limitations in S/4HANA Cloud for certain project types [3]. Ensure your project configuration aligns with supported overhead calculation scenarios for the BNA scope item.

entry.title: Can we use terraform to delete users who have left the organization at BTP level?

entry.title: WIP Revaluation in SAP Actual Costing (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)
entry.content:
This blog answers the following questions:

  • Is WIP revaluation relevant to your business?
  • How to ensure WIP is revaluated properly in Actual Costing?
  • What are WIP buildup and WIP reduction?
  • Example
  • How is WIP in Actual Costing different from WIP in Production Accounting?
  • Conclusion

Is WIP revaluation relevant to your business?

In a manufacturing business, it’s easy to focus only on the finished products rolling off the line. But there’s something else just as important: Work in process (WIP) - the unfinished items scattered across the production floor. The material components and the labor costs tied up in these in‑progress products all carry real value.

Before these items become finished goods and eventually show up as part of the cost of goods sold (COGS), their value is a key component of the balance sheet. Without Actual Costing, WIP materials and activities are valued at planned price (standard price). Similar to what is described in blog: Actual Costing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for revaluation of COGS and inventory, SAP Actual Costing revaluates WIP at actual price and ensures higher accuracy in legal and management reports.

In summary, WIP revaluation with SAP Actual Costing is relevant if:

  • You run a manufacturing business.
  • Your business has a significant amount of WIP on the production line at period end.
  • The prices of materials and activities consumed for production tend to fluctuate throughout the period.
  • You want to valuate these WIP materials and activities at actual price rather than standard price.

How to ensure WIP is revaluated properly in Actual Costing?

In Actual Costing, WIP is revaluated as part of consumption in Settlement step in actual costing runs (CKMLCP).

  • For materials, the price differences collected from receipts in the period are allocated to consumption and ending inventory proportionally by quantity.
  • Production activities are also revaluated based on consumed quantities and actual cost rates maintained in Manage Cost Rates – Actual (F3422) or Actual Cost Rate Calculation - Cost Centers (KSII).

As you can see, quantity calculation is crucial to ensure correct WIP revaluation. Therefore, before executing actual costing run (in CKMLCP), remember to let the system know the WIP quantities of materials and activities by executing the job “WIP Quantity Calculation for Actual Costing: Event-Based Order and PCC” in app Schedule Inventory Accounting Jobs (F2292).

For any materials or activities consumed in a production order, the system determines WIP quantities with the formula:

WIP quantity = Actual consumption - Target consumption, that is:

WIP quantity = Actual consumption quantity - Planned consumption quantity * (actual delivery quantity/planned delivery quantity)

If the calculated WIP quantity for a material or activity is negative, the system recognizes zero WIP quantity. No allocation to WIP will occur for it.

What are WIP buildup and WIP reduction?

As often seen in Material Price Analysis (CKM3N), “WIP Buildup” and “WIP Reduction” refer to the following:

  • WIP Build Up for Order <order ID>: Increase of WIP quantity in the current period.
  • WIP Reduction for Order <order ID> <previous period>: Reverse partial or full WIP quantity generated in previous period. This part of WIP quantity has been transformed into finished products and delivered to stock in current period.

Note: This reduction is based on a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) principle, meaning the oldest WIP layer is reduced first.

After the Settlement step in costing run (CKMLCP), the following new lines show up for above WIP quantity changes:

  • Revaluation of WIP Build Up <current period>: The price difference allocated to the WIP quantity increase in current period.
  • Revaluation of WIP Reduction <previous period>: The price difference allocated to the WIP quantity that is cumulated from previous period but is consumed into finished products in the current period.

Example

The following example shows how WIP is accumulated and reduced over a 3‑period production process, and how it is revaluated in each period.

Planned Material Components and Activities in Order

A production order (1011600) is created for manufacturing 100 PC of FG228 in period 2025/009. The planned input materials and activities are as follows:

Table 1: Planned Input Materials and Activities

Material/ActivityQuantity
RM16100 PC
RM27100 PC
Activity 1 (Machine)800 min (13.333 H)
Activity 3 (setup)120 min (2 H)
Activity 11 (Personnel)800 min (13.333 H)

You can also view the planned quantities in order itemization below:

Planned Material and Activity Consumption of Order 1011600

Material/Activity Consumption and Partial Delivery in Period 009

In period 2025/009, you withdraw materials and confirm activities for the production, and deliver finished products to stock as follows:

Table 2: Order Transactions in Period 009

Period

Material/Activity

Actual Consumption of Material/Activity

Target Consumption of Material/Activity

Delivery to Stock

009

RM16

60 PC

100 PC * (50 PC/100 PC) = 50 PC

/

 

RM27

40 PC

100 PC * (50 PC/100 PC) = 50 PC

/

 

Activity 1 (Machine)

300 min (5 H)

800 min * (50 PC/100 PC) = 6.667 H

/

 

Activity 3 (setup)

90 min (1.5 H)

2 H * (50 PC/100 PC) = 1 H

/

 

Activity 11 (Personnel)

300 min (5 H)

800 min * (50 PC/100 PC) = 6.667 H

/

 

FG228

/

 

50 PC

Target consumption is calculated using the formula:

Target consumption = Planned consumption quantity * (actual delivery quantity/planned delivery quantity)

You can verify the above actual quantities and target quantities in Order Cost Details – Event-Based (F4254):

Quantity and Cost Details of Order

Please ignore the rounding differences in minute-to-hour conversion. Also, activity 3 (Setup) is a lot-size-independent activity, hence the target quantity 2H (equal to plan quantity).

Calculate WIP Quantity of Production Order in Period 009

At period end, you run the job “WIP Quantity Calculation for Actual Costing: Event-Based Order and PCC” in F2292:

WIP Quantity Calculation Job

 The calculated WIP quantities can be viewed in Display WIP Quantities Document (COMLWIPDOC):

WIP Quantity Calculation Result of Period 009

 The system determines 10 PC of raw material RM16 and 0.5 hour of activity 3 (Setup) as WIP for the order 1011600 using the formula:

WIP quantity = Actual Consumption - Target consumption, i.e.

WIP quantity = Actual consumption quantity - Planned consumption quantity * (actual delivery quantity/planned delivery quantity)

Table 3: How WIP quantities are calculated for period 009?

Period

Material/Activity

Actual Consumption

Target Consumption (See table 2 for calculation)

WIP Quantity

009

RM16

60 PC

50 PC

60 PC – 50 PC = 10 PC

 

RM27

40 PC

50 PC

40 PC – 50 PC = -10 PC (negative). No WIP.

 

Activity 1 (Machine)

300 min (5 H)

6.667 H

Negative, no WIP

 

Activity 3 (setup)

90 min (1.5 H)

1 H

1.5 H - 1H = 0.5H

 

Activity 11 (Personnel)

300 min (5 H)

6.667 H

Negative, no WIP

View Actual Costing Settlement Result

Unlike other materials or activities, RM16 and activity 3 were partially consumed for completed production and partially for WIP production. Therefore, at the period end, when you settle their price differences in the actual costing run (CKMLCP), the price differences are allocated to “Production of FG228” and “WIP Build Up” in proportion of quantity as shown below:

RM16 WIP and Production Consumption in Period 009

 Activity 3 WIP and Production Consumption in Period 009

 As shown in Material Price Analysis (CKM3N) for the finished product FG228, only the price differences of non-WIP parts of RM16 and activity 3 are rolled up.

Quantity Structure of Product FG228 in Period 009

View Posted Revaluation Journal Entries

After executing Post Closing in actual costing run (CKMLCP), the following accounting journal entries are posted by Actual Costing. Both include the WIP revaluation lines.

WIP of RM16 in Accounting Journal Entry (Period 009)

 Journal entry 4700000057 is for all materials involved in the costing run:

  • Line 19: PRY (Price Difference - Material)

The price difference of 77.22 EUR is received for material RM16 and will be transferred to its WIP account.

  • Line 20: WPM (WIP from Price Difference - Material)

The price difference is debited to WIP account in order to bring the total WIP value in balance sheet to actual cost.

Activity 3 in Accounting Journal Entry (Period 009)

Journal entry 4700000059 is for all activities involved in the costing run:

  • Line 14: PRL (Price Difference - Activity)

The price difference of 0.21 EUR is received for activity 3, e.g. due to deviation between actual and planned cost rate of the activity. It will be transferred to the WIP account.

  • Line 15: WPA (WIP from Price Difference - Activity)

The price difference is debited to WIP account to bring the total WIP value in balance sheet to actual cost.

With that, you can ensure the balance sheet (and P&L) are updated with the real WIP values in period 009.     

More Material/Activity Consumption and Delivery in Period 010

Production continues in period 010. More activities/materials are consumed and finished products delivered:

Table 4: Order Transactions in Period 010

Period

Material/Activity

Actual Consumption of Material/Activity

Delivery to Stock

010

RM16

30 PC

/

 

RM27

50 PC

/

 

Activity 1 (Machine)

/

/

 

Activity 3 (setup)

/

/

 

Activity 11 (Personnel)

480 min (8 H)

/

 

FG228

/

30 PC

Calculate WIP Quantity of Production Order in Period 010

Till the end of 010, the product FG228 delivered to stock has increased to 80 PC. The new delivery and consumption in period 0101 change the WIP quantity of raw materials and activities to the following:

Table 5: How WIP quantities are calculated for period 010?

Period

Material/Activity

Actual Consumption of Material/Activity

Target Consumption of Material/Activity

Total WIP Quantity at End of Period 10

009 + 010

RM16

90 PC

100 PC * (80 PC/100 PC) = 80 PC

90 PC – 80 PC = 10 PC

 

RM27

90 PC

100 PC * (80 PC/100 PC) = 80 PC

90 PC – 80 PC = 10 PC

 

Activity 1 (Machine)

300 min (5 H)

800 min * (80 PC/100 PC) = 10.665 H

Negative, no WIP

 

Activity 3 (setup)

90 min (1.5 H)

2 H * (80 PC/100 PC) = 1.6 H

Negative, no WIP

 

Activity 11 (Personnel)

780 min (13 H)

800 min * (80 PC/100 PC) = 10.665 H

13 H - 10.665 H = 2.335 H

As we have WIP quantities (of RM16 and activity 3) in period 009, according to the FIFO principle, in the new period 010, these quantities should be consumed first, then followed by the new materials withdrawn and activities confirmed in period 010. Therefore, after you run the WIP quantity calculation job, the WIP quantity by period is updated to the following:

WIP Quantity Calculation Result of Period 010

 Calculation of WIP changes of both periods is detailed in the table below:

Table 6: WIP Quantity Changes of Period 009 and 010

Material/Activity

Current Total WIP Quantity

WIP Quantity at Period 9

WIP Change of Period 9

WIP Increase of Period 10

RM16

10 PC

10 PC

- 10 PC (WIP Reduction)

10 PC

RM27

10 PC

0

0 (No change)

10 PC

Activity 1 (Machine)

0

0

0 (No change)

0

Activity 3 (setup)

0

0.5H

-0.5 H (WIP Reduction)

0

Activity 11 (Personnel)

2.335 H

0

0 (No change)

2.335 H (WIP Buildup)

View Quantity Structure of FG228 of Period 010

The quantity structure of FG228 of period 010 indicates: to manufacture 30 PC of FG228 in period 010, how many quantities of materials and activities were actually consumed.

WIP Reduction

The 10 PC of RM16 and 0.5 H of activity 3 remaining in WIP accounts of period 009 are consumed in period 010, hence the WIP Reduction as highlighted below:

WIP Reduction Shown in Quantity Structure of FG228 (Period 010)

Important: Although these 2 WIP reduction lines are consumed for manufacturing F228 in period 010, their costs are calculated based on the determined price of period 009:

  • RM16 at price 7.97 EUR/PC of period 009 (in comparison, RM16 has the price 0.25 EUR/PC in period 010 as shown in the line above)
  • Activity 3 at price 8.50 EUR/hour of period 009
WIP Buildup and Consumption to Production

Besides the above highlighted WIP reduction, the new consumption in period 010 is divided into WIP buildup and production consumption as follows:

Table 7: WIP Buildup and Consumption to Production

Material/Activity

New Consumption in Period 010

 

WIP Quantities of Period 010 (as in table 5)

 

Consumed for Production of 30 PC FG228 in Period 010 (as in Qty Structure)

RM16

30 PC

=

10 PC

+

20 PC

RM27

50 PC

=

10 PC

+

40 PC

Activity 11 (Personnel)

8 H

=

2.335 H (WIP Buildup)

+

5.665 H

As shown in Material Price Analysis (CKM3N) of RM16 for period 010, the new consumption of 30 PC is divided into 20 PC of production consumption and 10 PC of WIP buildup (if you click button WIP, the WIP Reduction of Period 009 line can show up under Production).

WIP and Production Consumption of RM16 in Period 010

View Accounting Journal Entries of Period 010

After executing the Post Closing step in actual costing run (CKMLCP), we can check the accounting journal entries posted for WIP items.

WIP Reduction and buildup of RM16

Since RM16 has both WIP reduction and WIP buildup, we can take it as an example to examine the WIP-related journal entry lines:

WIP Reduction and Buildup of RM16 in Accounting Journal Entry (Period 010)

  • Pair of lines 17 and 18: Price difference of 77.22 EUR (PRY) is passed onto WIP increase/buildup (WPM debit) of period 010).

Note: This is similar to lines 25 and 26 for RM27, representing WIP increase in one period.

  • Pair of lines 19 and 20: Remaining WIP difference of 77.22 EUR of previous period 009 is deducted (WPM credit) in period 010 and offset by material WIP reduction (PRM).

Note: PRM is only relevant in cross-period WIP reduction.

WIP Reduction of Activity 3

Activity 3 only has a WIP reduction of 0.5 hours in period 010. As shown in the journal entry below, the cumulated WIP price difference of 0.21 EUR is decreased (WPA credit) and offset by activity WIP reduction (PRA).

WIP Reduction of Activity 3 in Accounting Journal Entry (Period 010)

Material Consumption and Final Delivery in Period 011

In period 011, more materials are withdrawn, and more products are delivered as below:

Table 8: Order Transactions in Period 011

Period

Material/Activity

Actual Consumption of Material/Activity

Delivery to Stock

011

RM16

35 PC

/

 

RM27

/

/

 

Activity 1 (Machine)

/

/

 

Activity 3 (setup)

/

/

 

Activity 11 (Personnel)

/

/

 

FG228

/

15 PC

The total delivery quantity reaches 95 PC (50 + 30 + 15) and we decide to set the production order to Technically Completed (TECO).  

With that, all remaining WIP from previous period 010 are consumed for the production of 15 PC in period 011. After we execute the WIP quantity calculation job, all WIP quantities of period 010 are cleared up. You can cross-check the WIP quantities with table 6.

WIP Quantity Calculation Result for Period 011

Taking RM16 as an example, quantities and values under WIP Production are cleared and moved to under Production in Material Price Analysis (CKM3N).

WIP Quantity Moved to Production for RM16 as Order is Completed

View Quantity Structure of FG228 of Period 011

As seen from the valuated quantity structure of FG228 in period 011, to produce 15 PC of FG228, all remaining WIP materials and activities from period 010, along with the new withdrawal of 35 PC of RM16 in period 011, are consumed.

WIP Reduction of Materials/Activities Shown in Quantity Structure of FG228 (Period 011)

View Accounting Journal Entries of Period 011

With the completion of Post Closing step, remaining WIP of RM16 from previous period 010 (WPM) is passed onto its WIP reduction (PRM), which is in turn, rolled up to product FG228 (PRY).

WIP Reduction of RM16 and Difference Rollup to FG228

That concludes our example of cross-period WIP buildup and reduction.

How is WIP in Actual Costing different from WIP in Production Accounting?

Working in manufacturing, you’re probably quite familiar with WIP value of production orders. You might wonder how it is different or related to the WIP revaluation we explained above. It can be summarized as follows:

Table 9: Comparison Between WIP in Production Accounting and WIP in Actual Costing

 

WIP in Production Accounting

WIP Revaluation in Actual Costing

Valuated Object

Production order, process order, product cost collector

Materials/activities consumed for production in a period.

Note: Materials must have a price determination 3 (Single/Multi-level).

Definition

Value of unfinished products

Value difference of consumed materials/activities that haven’t resulted in finished products

Calculation Method

Difference between debit and credit of an order

WIP quantity multiplied by price difference

Calculation Formula

WIP value = Actual cost (debit) - Goods receipt value (credit), that is:

WIP = Standard material cost + standard activity cost + overhead cost - goods receipt value of manufactured product

Note: All materials, activities, and products are valuated at standard price.

Note: Calculation for product cost collectors is slightly different than above. See https://help.sap.com/s4_ce_prd_acc.

WIP revaluation value of each material/activity = WIP quantity * (actual price - standard price), where “WIP quantity” is calculated based on all orders of the period with the formula:

WIP quantity = Actual consumption quantity - Planned consumption quantity * (actual delivery quantity/planned delivery quantity)

Calculation Timepoint

Automatic and event-based (whenever goods movement or activity confirmation is performed)

WIP quantity calculation is manually triggered by job “WIP Quantity Calculation for Actual Costing: Event-Based Order and PCC” in F2292.

WIP revaluation is done with Settlement step in actual costing run (CKMLCP).

Period 009 Example

WIP value is:

-1120.81 EUR = (goods issue of 45 EUR + activity confirmation of 391.86 EUR + overhead of 42.33 EUR) – goods receipt of 1600 EUR.

Note: In the accounting journal entry posted by Production Accounting, the value is posted with the following dimensions:

  • Order 1011600
  • Product FG228
  • G/L account: Configured in SSCUI Event-Based WIP and Variance Posting (103940)
  • Reference Transaction: EBWP

WIP revaluation of RM16 is:

77.22 EUR = WIP quantity of 10 PC * (actual price of 7.97 EUR – standard price of 0.25 EUR).

Note: In the accounting journal entry posted by Actual Costing, the value is posted with the following dimensions

  • Order: Empty (sum of all orders in the period)
  • Product: RM16
  • G/L account: Configured for transaction key WPM in SSCUI Automatic Account Determination (100297)
  • Reference Transaction: MLHD

WIP revaluation of activity 3 is:

0.21 EUR = WIP quantity of 0.5 H * (actual cost rate of 8.50 EUR – planned cost rate of 8.08 EUR).

Note: In the accounting journal entry posted by Actual Costing, the value is posted with the following dimensions

  • Order: Empty (sum of all orders in the period)
  • Product: Empty
  • Activity: Empty
  • G/L account: Configured for transaction key WPA in SSCUI Automatic Account Determination (100297)
  • Reference Transaction: MLHD

The following screens are provided for you to verify the WIP calculation explained in the above table:

Cost Details of Order 1011600 in Period

The above screen from Order Cost Details – Event Based (F4254) shows the business transactions occur on the production order 1011600 till end of period 009. Based on that, the WIP value (-1120.81 EUR) is determined by summing up all incurred costs and then deducting the goods receipt value.

WIP Revaluation of RM16 in Period 009

 The above screen shows in Material Price Analysis (CKM3N), the WIP revaluated for RM16 is 77.22 EUR. It’s determined by multiplying the WIP quantity (10 PC) by the difference between actual price (7.97 EUR) and standard price (0.25 EUR) of RM16.

Note: Actual price of RM16 is only available after executing Settlement step in Actual Costing. Before that, only the standard WIP value (2.50 EUR = 10 PC * 0.25 EUR/PC) is available, as shown in Preliminary Valuation (PrelimVal) column. WIP Revaluation of Activity 3 in Period 009

Similarly, the above screen shows in Activity Consumption Analysis (CKM3A), the WIP revaluated for activity 3 is 0.21 EUR. It’s determined by multiplying the WIP quantity (0.5 H) by the difference between actual cost rate (8.50 EUR) and planned cost rate (8.08 EUR) of activity 3.

Note: Actual cost rate is read from F3422 or KSII, whichever is the latest. The Settlement step of Actual Costing revaluates the WIP.

In summary, Production Accounting calculates standard WIP value for individual orders, while Actual Costing revaluates the WIP by using the actual price/cost rate on material/activity level. By combining both, you can achieve a more accurate WIP value, and a better view of how well your production and inventory operations are running.

Conclusion

This blog explores when WIP matters for your business and how SAP Actual Costing revalues it with a cross‑period production example. By understanding the mechanics behind WIP calculation and revaluation, you can turn cost transparency into a real operational advantage.

entry.title: Re: Order Type YA01 marked as read only or during order c...
entry.content:
Hello @Abhijit_Muley 

Thank you for your question.

In public cloud, you should rather control your process/configuration than using field-control BAdI for your described scenario.

EAM_ORDER_FIELD_CONTROL is not suitable for pre-creation restriction of order type selection.

The public cloud offers either YA01 or YA05 as standard phases-enabled order types and process governance.

See references below for guidance.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/5fbd47786341411992fc9915284da2b2.html

You can enforce YA01 by configuring the notification-to-order process, so creation from notifications follows the Reactive Maintenance path and ensure you align roles/process governance, so your users do not operate alternative type paths. This ensures you are consistent with the defined cloud compliance in ensuring consistent order type behaviour when you create order from notifications.

The practical way is:

  • Default mapping between notification type YA01 and its assigned order type
  • Make sure you do not offer other combinations to your users

So, you trying to carry out a pre-create lock dropdown in the same popup BAdI is not the typical approach to consider. 

See similar question and response here: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/notification-types-in-sap-s-4-hana-cloud-public-edition/qaq-p/14059203

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: FAGL_FCV-Posting period 002 2026 is not open
entry.content:
Hi @Thayalan ,
My friend the error you’re seeing in FAGL_FCV (“Posting period 002 2026 is not open”) typically arises because the posting period for the non-leading ledger is not open in transaction OB52. By default, many configurations only open periods for the leading ledger, so when you run foreign currency valuation, the system checks the ledger group and finds that the non-leading ledger’s period isn’t open.

To resolve this please follow the steps related to SPRO/OB52

1.Check Ledger Group Assignment
Go to SPRO → Financial Accounting (New) → Financial Accounting Global Settings (New) → General Ledger Accounting (New) → Periodic Processing → Valuate → Check assignment of Accounting Principle to Ledger Group. Ensure the correct accounting principle is assigned to the ledger group that includes your non-leading ledger.

2. Assign Valuation Area and Accounting Principle
In the same SPRO path, verify that the valuation area is correctly linked to the accounting principle. This ensures the valuation runs for the right ledger group.

3. Open Posting Periods in OB52
Transaction OB52 controls posting periods. You need to open the period for the ledger group that includes your non-leading ledger (not just 0L). If you only opened periods for the leading ledger, the non-leading one will throw this error.

4. Check Valuation Configuration
In FAGL_FCV, confirm that the valuation area is assigned to the correct ledger group. If you intend valuation only for the leading ledger, adjust the configuration so the non-leading ledger isn’t included.

As you resolve the issue please accept my solution.

entry.title: Re: SAP R/3 MIRO: Installment Payment Terms vs. Net Docum...
entry.content:
Hi @Muratoztrk ,
My friend this is a standard SAP R/3 restriction, installment payment terms cannot be used with Net document type invoices in MIRO, and invoices posted without the Net indicator cannot be reversed in MIRO.

Following compliant solution might help

If reversal via MIRO is required → Use Net document type with normal (non-installment) payment terms. This ensures audit-compliant reversals.
If installment terms are mandatory → Post the invoice in MIRO without the Net indicator, but handle reversals in FI (FB08) instead of MIRO. SAP recommends this as the supported path.
Alternative → Split the PO or invoice into two items (75% and 25%) so MIRO posts two vendor line items without needing installment terms. This avoids the Net/Installment conflict and keeps reversals possible.

What I am trying to say is, SAP recommends either using standard payment terms with Net documents for MIRO reversals, or handling installment terms outside MIRO (via FI or invoice splitting).

Hope you got it.

entry.title: Re: Currency translation question in BPC 10.1 Standard on NW
entry.content:
Hi @srikanth_ramachandran ,
My friend in BPC 10.1 Standard, you’re right that only one currency type dimension is allowed, so you can’t directly store multiple transaction currencies in the rates model. So the usual workaround is as mentioned below
Load balances with a transaction currency identifier (your user-defined dimension).
Create separate rate records per transaction currency by modeling each TC as a “pseudo-entity” or “pseudo-rate type” in the rates model. For example, GBP and JPY would each have their own rate entity entry with rates to EUR.
Translate in two steps as below.
From TC → LC (EUR) using the pseudo-rate entity.
From LC → reporting currencies (USD, etc.) using the standard rate entity.

By this way, BPC knows which rate to apply because the TC dimension drives selection of the correct pseudo-rate entity. Essentially, you’re extending the rates model design to hold multiple TC→LC mappings, then leveraging the standard LC→reporting currency translation.

Let me know if you have any more queries .

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Downtime-optimized Upgrades and Hotfix Collection Imports for Product
entry.content:
Hi @Atul_Joshi85 

1) We plan to deliver a feature in Landscape Portal, that the start of the technical downtime can be specifically set by the customer (in a 2h time frame within the announced 24h maintenance window). We plan to offer this new feature for release 2605. 

2) The upgrade process ensures, that custom lifecycle activities (like import software components) are not supported during the whole process

3) Same as 2). In SAP BTP ABAP Environment, only the customer itself decides on deployment of partner products and their patches.  The partner cannot push patches etc. into customer systems directly.
